title: Inhaler & Spacer Technique: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Inhaler & Spacer Technique
Identify key diagnostic features of Inhaler & Spacer Technique
Apply clinical reasoning to Inhaler & Spacer Technique management
Recognize common pitfalls in Inhaler & Spacer Technique

sections.title: Overview of Inhaler & Spacer Technique
sections.content:
**Inhaler & Spacer Technique**



**Key Points:**
• MDI (metered-dose inhaler) requires coordination of actuation and inhalation
• Spacer/VHC eliminates need for coordination, increases lung deposition from ~10% to ~20%
• DPI (dry powder inhaler) requires forceful inhalation, no spacer needed

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• MDI (metered-dose inhaler) requires coordination of actuation and inhalation
• Spacer/VHC eliminates need for coordination, increases lung deposition from ~10% to ~20%

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• DPI (dry powder inhaler) requires forceful inhalation, no spacer needed
• Technique assessment: shake MDI, exhale fully, slow deep breath with actuation, hold 10 seconds
• Common errors: not shaking, breathing too fast, not using spacer, forgetting to hold breath

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Inhaler & Spacer Technique:**

• DPI (dry powder inhaler) requires forceful inhalation, no spacer needed
• Technique assessment: shake MDI, exhale fully, slow deep breath with actuation, hold 10 seconds
• Common errors: not shaking, breathing too fast, not using spacer, forgetting to hold breath

options.explanation: MDI (metered-dose inhaler) requires coordination of actuation and inhalation

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Inhaler & Spacer Technique. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Spacer/VHC eliminates need for coordination, increases lung deposition from ~10% to ~20%
options.explanation: Spacer/VHC eliminates need for coordination, increases lung deposition from ~10% to ~20%

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.
